sentences.text: Myself, I had many friends in the ghetto.
That's-- that's normal.
Many friend, many schoolmates.
There are many those-- of my friends-- have left the ghetto in earlier times, and they went to the partisans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- Mostly, the fact that I was working in [NON-ENGLISH], I was able to-- I had a very good communication with the outside.

Because across the street of [NON-ENGLISH], which is the factory marmalade, was the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a-- a place of the largest milk product place, where they used to make butter and cheese, and so far and so on.
So I used to-- I found a way through underground from [NON-ENGLISH], where the pipes, the sewer pipes were, and some other type of pipes.
And I used to climb through these pipes and reach the other side of the street, and come in there.
And I used to have a-- I befriended a Lithuanian, who used to give me butter and some other things.
And I used to bring them to the ghetto.
A few times they caught me, and they would beat me up, but it was not terrible.
And once, the head of this [NON-ENGLISH] company was a German, a civilian, but he was very, very close to the Gestapo.
And he caught me taking some marmalade.
And he pointed out to me that next time, if he catches me, he's just going to take me to the Gestapo.
And that would mean the end.
But to, you know, forced to survive, you did anything you were able to.
Sometimes, I used to-- I was blondish when I was younger.
And I used to take off my-- everybody had to carry-- I mean, to wear sewed in Jewish stars from the front and from the back.
They had to be sewed in.
They couldn't be just stapled in or something.
So I used to take them off from time to time.
And I used to go out and communicate.
And I had a good relationship with certain people.
And I used to help some of my friends who went to the partisans.
And I was pretty aware of what's happening, what's going on.
My uncle was-- and I listened to him because he was a leader in the community at that time.
And he just didn't want me to go to the partisans because many of my friends, some of my friends were killed.
It was extremely dangerous, and he felt that him and my grandmother, who, I was like the idol for her.
So I tried to work from inside and tried to be helpful in a certain way.
But it came already to a point where I saw that the ghetto is being liquidated.
Although many people felt, especially with what's happening on the front, with the German defeat, and all the political atmosphere have changed.
And we saw also that-- and we became-- in 1943, we became a concentration camp.
Because, before that, we used to be watched by Wehrmacht, by the-- by the-- just by the military.
Now we were assigned to the SS, and they were terrible.
We used to have Hungarian SS people who were terrible.
They were even worse than the Germans.
We used to have SS people from the Russians, from Ukrainians, from the Vlasov's army who were Russians, who helped the German, and who were aligned and affiliated with the Germans.
And they were also terrible.
So we came, and myself, with some of the friends.
It was already, at that time, that was '43.
I was like 19 already.
And we decided-- we had a small group.
Within the family and some friends, we decided that we are going to try to plan a escape.
We used to come out through the-- there was a gate, a special gate where we used to come out and in.
First of all, when it happened to the children, when they were taken out, we had under the house, we built a home-- home built bunker.
And my cousin, which was already three years old at that time-- my uncle's name was Vaikovich, And that was Shulamit Vaikovich.
That was my uncle's and my aunt's daughter.
She was-- all the time, we tried to save her.
And when we saw that they took out the children, and so we knew that we were still able to hold onto her.
We knew that one of the days-- I mean, she's going to be taken away.
So there was a Jewish rabbi's wife who used to go out and commute and communicate with the outside Lithuanians.
And she had some Lithuanians who were, for a certain price, they will take Jewish children, and hide them under cover.
And this was with my uncle's daughter, which he was also friendly.
My uncle was the head of the bread supply department.
He was very straight and very careful.
And the two heads of the camp which where SS, which was-- one of them was Pfiffiger.
The name Pfiffiger, he was a terrible guy.
But there was a other guy that was a little bit better.
And he was in civilian life in Germany.
Before he became SS, he was actually drafted to this, as he was not a too young man.
And he indicated certain sympathy.
And he was in very good relations, so my uncle put his life in his hands.
And there was Pfiffiger and [NON-ENGLISH].
Pfiffiger was the nice guy, and [NON-ENGLISH] was the devil.
And I'll explain to you why he was the devil.
So he met with him and he told him that he has his daughter.
He told him I have here the daughter-- my daughter, and I'm sure that you also have children.
And you know, the tides was already changing.
And the chairman, some of them who had a little bit more foresight, they saw that Hitler is not going to go too long.
And so he tried to befriend my uncle.
And he told him that's he's going to try to help him out, help him in carrying out this.
And my uncle told him the story.
He put it plain.
He said, look, if it's going to happen, it's going to happen.
We have no choice.
So they used to bring in the bread with two horses and carriages.
And then they used to take out the empty bags out from the ghetto in order to reload them.
And so we gave-- I gave my cousin a sleeping pill, which the German gave it to us in order to give her.
And we put her under the bags, and we brought her out through the gates.
And there, she met this lady, this rabbi's wife, turned her over to a Lithuanian family.
There was another child with us, and was also turned over.
And unfortunately, the older child was turned over to the Germans, and they killed him.
So you never-- it was like a lottery.
You didn't know who was genuine, and who is going-- just going to take the money and then take the life of the child.

So we decided through some way-- and by cutting down the ghetto, by shrinking, there were a number of houses which were left outside of the ghetto, within the perimeter of the general surroundings, but it was not-- they didn't watch that so much.
Because that was actually considered outside, because it used to be the ghetto, but then they deleted this part.
And then they put a fence, and still the fence remains over this circle around.
But still, we had-- the real ghetto was a little bit farther, about 600, 700 meters farther.
So what we decided, when we went to work, that one of us will bribe the guard.
And they were some guards who you were able to bribe them.
And didn't need too much money for doing that.
We gave them some certain things.
And we used to bring in-- when we used to come from the city-- we bring in some-- we concentrated on getting some sugar, getting water and bread.
This was the three items that you can survive with.
And we brought it.
In one of these abandoned houses, we used to, every time, to come in and to leave certain things.
And then we used to go to the ghetto.
And then, one day, we were actually five people.
It was my aunt's nephew, who was like 30 years old.
It was a older man by the name of Shayka Fin, Fin was his last name, who was working together-- his uncle was working to the ghetto with my uncle in the supply of the bread.
There was a older man, by the name of Fakturovskii.
And there was a young man who was actually from Poland.
And he came as a refugee, and he was taken in to the ghetto.
So we were actually five.
We decided that, before anything, we would explore the-- get to this house where we had the supplies, and we will explore through this-- in the second floor, and we used the 13-year-old boy.
I mean, he was our lookout to see in time when the guard is-- how often the guard is moving.
And we had also wire cutters.
And it was a dark night.
Not stars or the moon.
And we waited three or four days.
We were still there in the house.
We run out.
And, for some reason, the guards, when they counted the people, they were-- they were not-- truthfully, they were not as strict as like six or seven or eight months ago.
They already became concerned with their own fate.
They thought that-- they-- they thought a little bit more about their own fate.
The matter of Jews became already secondary in a certain way.

So we timed the guards' time, and we cut out-- in the middle of the night, when it was quite dark, and the guard was making his run, we just cut through the fence, and we run to the next closest neighborhood.
In order to-- because nighttime, we wouldn't be able to go too far, because they would spot us right away.
We saw already-- also, previously, there were many ambulances and many wounded German soldiers who were brought from the front to Lithuania too, and there were taken to the hospital.
Now, we slept over there.
And then, in the morning, when the sun came out, we start moving.
And we moved like in a-- in a way that we were one behind the other one, was about a few feet away.
We should not be together, considered together.
And we walked through the day.
And we tried to avoid-- we tried to walk out from the city and to go towards [NON-ENGLISH], which was a forest.
And one of us, this Fin, knew the neighborhood because he used to live in [NON-ENGLISH].
And he know exactly where we were going.
Plus the fact that we knew that there were some partisans, some freedom fighters in the vicinity.
When we came to a point, it was already towards the other half in the afternoon, we came to a point where we had to cross a main highway.
And this was a very dangerous point because there was movement of Germans.
The Germans wouldn't know the distinguish between Jew and non-Jew.
Plus the fact we didn't have way-- we actually-- they could have spotted us in the face.
But we tried to camouflage ourselves in a certain way.
But some of the Lithuanians with a-- two of them, one of them was with a-- with a bicycle.
And the other one was on foot and they had their rifles.
And one of them came over and they saw us and said, documents.
When he said that, we were already outside of the city.
We just disbanded and start running.
This guy with a rifle was not able to shoot towards-- so he pointed the one apparently he had one-- this guy that was from Poland.
And then we lost each other.
I mean, we didn't know-- I didn't know what happened.
I remember that I was running like never in my life, you know.
And some bullets crossed my path, but I run.
And it was a very-- a corn field.
And run as fast as I was able to, and then I threw myself in the corn field in order to just lay for a little while.
And now that they have called this guy-- threw his bicycle apparently.
First of all, he went to the guy to inquire the guy that he heard.
And apparently, they shot him.
That's what we found out a little bit later.
And the other friends of mine, I just didn't know what actually happened.
I know exactly what happened to me.
They have tried to search the area with horses, but, fortunately, there was also a luck, a little bit of luck, and, apparently, they have not spotted me.
Otherwise, I wouldn't be here.
So then nighttime, I start moving a little bit farther.
And I saw some flickering lights.
And I start moving towards the flickering lights.
And then I found myself in a little-- a little-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] forest.
And then I prepared-- it was cold.
It was, I think, April or May, and nighttime was very cold.
So what I did is I cut some grass with my hands.
And there was a little bit-- like in the river.
But very little river.
And this was gave me source.
I had my hat, and I used my hat to drink some water.
And I was very hungry and I almost ate grass or something.
And then part of the grass, I used to keep that, and then I stayed there for a day.
Next day, I heard voices.
And the voices were in Russian.
And Russian was my-- almost my native language.
So I didn't know what to do, whether to respond.
And I saw that a woman came with a pile of cloth.
And she start there-- in the little river, she started to wash them.
And then I came out, and I was so hungry that I felt that I put my life in my hands.
And I responded to her in Russian.
And I asked her-- she, first, she was shocked when she saw me.
I'm sure I looked like a devil, not like a person, you know, at that time, because, you know, from the all day long and from being there on the ground.
So I explained to her that I am Jewish and that I'm hungry, and that I'm trying to reach the forest, [NON-ENGLISH].
And she told me that this is a concentration camp, but not a watch camp.
It was from civilians, from civilian Russians.
And she told me that they have few Russian POWs who are hiding in the camp.
And they also are getting ready to join the freedom fighters.
But in meantime, she told-- I told her I'm hungry.
She told me in a few minutes, she's going to be back and bring me something to eat.
So I figured one of the two.
Either she's going to turn me in, or she's going to bring.
About half an hour later, she came and had a bottle of milk, a piece of bread, black bread, and a piece of meat.
And she gave to me-- and this was something to me like-- after two days not having anything to eat, I mean, it was really very rewarding.
She told me also something interesting.
She said, look, I'm not going to announce or tell anybody except these guys, this friend of mine, that you're escapee.
And at night time, next day, they might join you.
And they have some-- they have knives and they have some other things to defending.
And she brought me a knife also.
And about a day and a half or two days later, came two Russian POWs.
And we have identified our self, and they told us they are going to-- they know-- they had a little map, handmade map.
And they said that I should follow them if I wanted.

And this was-- a little by little, during the night time, we used to go watching our self, because during the day was very dangerous, you know, and especially in the morning time, when the sun came out.
We moved for about three days and we reached the forest.
There, one of them had a assignment ready, apparently.

And there were also-- we met there a group of people who were, first of all, freedom fighters, who were very much concerned about us because they-- every time they see new faces, they were suspicious that should not be some Germans spies or anything.
So there is where we met Vladimir, who was actually in charge.
And he told us that in the next day or something, we're going to get some supplies, and that we have to do some sabotage work, especially where the railroad is.
And this is, he says, it's a dangerous mission.
We have to put in some explosive.
And put them-- just put them, and then he is going to do with his men whatever worked out.
So we carried-- we had some bundles, you know, in Russian-- in camouflage bags.
And we distributed that in the meadow the next day.
Now, suddenly, this was going on for about two weeks, three weeks.
It was-- nobody had a exact date or timing.
We didn't have any watches, we just watched the sun.
And this was actually our guide.
And matter of fact, in about twenty some days passed, and, suddenly, Vladimir told us that we should be aware because something is-- pretty soon, there are going to be some battles in the vicinity.
And precisely, we put our ears to the ground we heard tanks moving.
And we didn't know which tanks or who was moving.
But then we found-- we saw from the farm, we saw the insignia of the German tanks.
Like six tanks passed about I would say 500, 600 yards from us.
And then, close to the evening, when the sundown, became a-- like a-- like the war-- like the front was right next to us.
They were start shooting and heavy artillery from all parts.
We saw burning bushes and we saw explosions in the vicinity.
And it was really like a nightmare.
And it lasted for about a couple hours.
Then, suddenly, everything quieted down, and it was like nothing happened.
Quite.
And then in a matter of hours, we heard Russian voices.
And this was the Russian-- the first Russian troops.
They called that the-- in Russian, it's [RUSSIAN], this is the front.
Pardon?
Reconnaissance.
Reconnaissance troops.
There was eight people.
Pardon?
They had communication.
They had the runners who were between the different outposts.
They used to send them down with small planes.
They used a parachute.
They used to send explosives and also some food and some rifle.
We had already-- each of us had a rifle and we were pretty well armed, you know.
We had also grenades and some for protection.
The first question was when they-- when-- when the first Russian-- I mean, we really didn't know what to do in that way.
First of all, they-- they were shocked when they saw us.
But no question in my mind, they didn't have any knowledge that they were going to find us.
But, apparently, in their road, in their way, they found different groups.
Especially when I identified myself-- and amazingly through the whole years, I carried a birth certificate, which I have that here, which is like five-- four by five inches.
And I carried that in my shoes through the whole years of the ghetto and everything.
And I was able to show them.
And this was a incredible identification for them, where they saw in Lithuanian and Russian.
I took it out in 1940.
They gave everyone that wanted to have a birth certificate.
They gave.
And this was a big help, because, truthfully, the only way to identify myself was that I took off my shoe and I pull it out.
And when he read, he was sure that I am Jewish and that I'm not going to betray them.
And the first question was whether we saw some German tanks.
And we saw some German troops moving.
And we mentioned to him we saw those tanks.
We saw those tanks went in one direction, one way.
That's it.
They destroyed them.
And the first thing when he took us, first of all, he separated me from the rest of the group.
Because he was more inquisitive with the Russians and the POWs, because they had a very big suspicion.
Because many POWs-- POWs were betrayed in many cases.
So me, he gave like royal treatment.
Beside that-- next to the-- When the front got a little closer, there were two Jewish-- there was a Jewish captain an a Jewish lieutenant.
And they, right away, when we identified our self, they told me right away that I should not stay here.
I should go more to the east because they say the front is very, very uncertain.
It might shift from one place to the other one.
And he says you have suffered plenty in your life.
There's no reason that after you are now safe, you should not be-- I mean, you should not give up your life.
Of course, later on, I found out it was in the Russian army, there were many anti-Semites also.

But this group, especially those people who are the front runners, who are in the sense-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they have the reconnaissance, they are much more human than some other troops where I served later.
They truthfully were really concerned with us, and they gave us a Studebaker, which they had later on.
Everything, the Jeeps and Studebakers, and the food was made in the USA.
Most of those-- many people don't know that.
That was worked out, was between Roosevelt and Stalin that the land lease.
And you already heard about that.
The Russian, yeah.
Then they send us a little bit farther down.
And in the matter of a week, they stormed in.
They came into Kovno and they took over.
They liberated Kovno.
And my first intention was I run to the ghetto.
I run to the place where I used to live.
The ghetto was destroyed.
Most of the houses were exploded.
And the bunkers were exploded.
And we found-- I found some people coming out from one of the bunkers when we came back.
I mean, it was-- we were amazed.
We didn't know how they survived.
And then I found some photos, family photos, which I had in the hiding place, where we used to hide my cousin.
And it was really-- I mean, this meeting, the Russian soldiers, it was such a feeling that it was beyond explanation.
It's like you see angels coming down.
We actually kissed the ground when we saw them.
And it was-- it was great.
Of course, I-- subsequently, I joined the Russian army, but I will give you more-- explain to you in more detail--
After I was in Kovno, I-- there was-- they had a draft for-- they drafted all the people at my age approximately.
And there was medical supervision, and there were many Jews who were actually in charge of that now, because most of the doctors were Jewish.
And my destiny was to go the army.
So rather than to be drafted, there was a lieutenant by the name of Gusev, who came to see us, especially those people who spoke Russian, Lithuanian, and German.
And he kind of liked me.
And the fact I had my diploma from the-- which I also saved from the gymnasium, which I folded down to-- and I had that with me.
And he said to me that he was going to talk with his-- with his immediate superior to take me into this special unit, which I became part of.
